food_subgroup: Mushrooms and fungi, cooked with soy-based sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a term for Pleurotus eryngii (king oyster mushroom) as a raw ingredient; no exact term exists for the prepared soy-sauce dish. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mushrooms and other fungi — cultivated, cooked
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for king oyster mushrooms in soy sauce. A00LC (cultivated mushrooms, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this prepared mushroom dish. SNOMED CT is not applicable here.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of king oyster mushrooms in soy sauce is approximately 150 g, representing a side dish portion as commonly served in a Chinese restaurant or zi char setting.

nutrient_source.title: Mushrooms, oyster, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11267 / FDC ID 169403); adapted with soy sauce seasoning contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central 'Soy sauce made from soy (tamari)' FDC ID 172238
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for king oyster mushrooms in soy sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. USDA SR Legacy cooked oyster mushroom values (FDC ID 169403) are used as the primary macro base. King oyster mushrooms (Pleurotus eryngii) have a nutritional profile closely comparable to oyster mushrooms (Pleurotus ostreatus) per published literature. Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce contribution (~400 mg/100 g for the finished dish). All other macros are taken directly from the USDA cooked oyster mushroom entry and represent a defensible estimate for this preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mushrooms — analogous estimate based on published GI values for cooked mushrooms (GI ~15) from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for king oyster mushrooms in soy sauce. Mushrooms as a food category have a consistently low GI (~15) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). The soy sauce component contributes negligible glycemic load. GL per serving = (15 × 6.1 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.4 (rounded), based on 150 g serving and 6.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that the soy sauce seasoning contributes approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g; a 150 g serving provides around 630 mg sodium, representing over 25% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, and portion control or requesting reduced-sodium preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: King oyster mushrooms in soy sauce have a low glycemic index (~15) and provide only 6.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 1.4 per serving, making this dish a suitable vegetable side for individuals managing blood glucose levels in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, cholesterol-free, with beneficial dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), contains no cholesterol, and provides 2.0 g dietary fibre per 100 g, which supports cardiovascular health; however, the elevated sodium from soy sauce warrants moderation for individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: King Oyster Mushrooms in Soy Sauce (豉香杏鲍菇, Chǐ xiāng xìng bào gū) is a popular Chinese vegetable dish featuring Pleurotus eryngii — the king oyster or king trumpet mushroom — cooked in a savory soy-based sauce. The mushrooms are typically sliced into thick rounds or batons, then stir-fried or braised with soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, a small amount of sugar, and sesame oil until tender and glossy. The result is a dish with a silky, meaty texture and a deeply umami, balanced savory flavor. King oyster mushrooms are prized for their firm, dense flesh that holds its shape during cooking and absorbs sauces well, giving them a satisfying mouthfeel often compared to scallops or abalone. Nutritionally, this dish is low in calories (~42 kcal/100 g), low in fat, and provides a moderate amount of plant protein (3.3 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g). The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to soy sauce seasoning (~420 mg/100 g). The glycemic index is very low (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. This dish is vegetarian and dairy-free, though it contains soy and gluten allergens from the soy sauce. It is commonly served as a side dish in Chinese zi char restaurants and home cooking across Singapore and China, and is well-suited as a low-GI, low-fat vegetable accompaniment in a balanced meal.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for king oyster mushrooms in soy sauce was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked oyster mushroom data (FDC ID 169403), which is the closest defensible analogue for Pleurotus eryngii. Sodium is estimated upward to account for soy sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mushrooms as a category. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input 'Fish and seafood' to 'Vegetables' — king oyster mushrooms are fungi/vegetables, not seafood; the input classification was erroneous. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked oyster mushroom (FDC ID 169403) as the closest approved analogue for Pleurotus eryngii; sodium is adjusted upward (~420 mg/100 g) to reflect soy sauce seasoning in the finished dish. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked oyster mushrooms. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked oyster mushrooms. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked oyster mushrooms. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for this plant-based preparation; no animal fats are used. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as mushrooms contain no dietary cholesterol. 8. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked oyster mushrooms (heat-labile vitamin C is negligible after cooking). 9. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as mushrooms are a plant-based food containing no vitamin B12. 10. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for mushrooms from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for king oyster mushrooms in soy sauce was located. 11. is_vegan set to false because oyster sauce (蚝油) is a common ingredient in this dish; a vegan version substituting mushroom sauce is possible but not the default preparation. 12. Price data from field survey data (SGD 17.94) is preserved as contextual metadata but not included in the nutrient record as it is not a nutritional field.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_group from erroneous 'Fish and seafood' to 'Vegetables'.

Built comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language with full consistency check performed.
Sourced nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169403) as closest approved analogue; adjusted sodium for soy sauce contribution.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) with explicit reasons in notes.
Documented all 0.0 values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg) with confirmed zero justifications in notes.
Set GI to 15 (numeric >0) with analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database; GL calculated as 1.4 for 150 g serving.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
Set ontology match_type values exclusively to allowed values: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
